author: @gurglejug627
text:
Worth knowing: All across the EU waste veggy oil is added to Diesel fuel by mandate - it's a totally daft idea as most people are still unaware of it, and any Diesel tank left for any length of time will grow 'Diesel bug' bacteria which clogs the whole system up. In fact it's very dangerous as it can cause sudden engine stoppages, to the extent that it's not added to the marine Diesel available in yacht marinas (which is why that is usually more expensive). 
      A better idea is for people who know how to use it properly (and who are aware of its ins and outs) run older engines on some 95% to 100% WVO biofuel and prolong their lifetime, which have very, very few particulate emissions.  
      The present daft idea also means people have to buy special additive bacteriacides and add them to their fuel inducing cost and pollution. But then, there's no helping bureaucrats and people who have spent all too much time at university ;)

author: @paramaniacwolverine843
text:
I've been running my 1985 Mitsubishi Pajero on WVO since about 2018. My mix ranges from 100% to 50/50 with petrol, diesel or paraffin. Being in South Africa I tend to drop down to 50/50 in winter.
This cleaning technique is great. Never saw it before and worth a look.
My oil was so clean that it looked almost like new oil. My prefilter was rags layed into cheap mesh sieves. Then it was pumped through a hydraulic tank filter with a hand drum pump into my first tank. From there I used an electric pump to push it through a 1 micro filter. I had a few thousand liters to filter, so this was the easiest and cheapest process I could come up with.
As for getting oil. That dried up many years ago. Luckily I filtered and squirrelled oil away.
There was a time when I literally had oil thrown at me, but today they use mainly Palm oil and sell their used oil back to the supplier.
Right now I'm researching black diesel.

author: @TonyJackson-cf3er
text: I've been making the 'real' biodiesel for some 15yrs; by transesterification of the waste veg oil; KOH & Methanol. I don't need to 'clean' my veg oil; I just sieve it. My 'hands on time' is about 3hrs all in making each 100lt batch. I buy my methanol in bulk and pay between 80p & £1 a litre. Last time I looked, paraffin is a lot more expensive. I can't see this method being less expensive than my current method and I'm unlikely to change process but it is interesting & I'll have a look into the process you've outlined. I'd be cautious about the water content in your 'paraffin veg oil mix' putting it through injectors in my cars though... My finished B100 is around 48p/litre.

replies.author: @__WJK__
replies.text: Agree, however, the least expensive, most hassle-free way I've found to process waste veg oil, is to simply pump the wvo through a 5 - 10 micron string-wound water filter, using a slightly modified engine oil pump (operated by a high-quality cordless or electric drill). The slightly modified engine oil pump, just about has the strength to suck a golf ball thru a garden hose. After that, the fwvo can be: 1) Mixed with diesel in a 50/50 blend (which btw will not gel at temps above 35deg), or 2) Can be run straight/undiluted, in a heated fuel tank, which allows the fwvo to be run year round, even in cold northern climates :^)

author: @thesetruths1404
text:
All of the used oils and hydraulic fluids I collect at home I catch as clean as possible in a catch can I keep sealed in double trash bags. When full, I pour the 15 qts into 5-qt oil jugs I save from new oil/fluid changes.  I let the jugs sit for at least 90 days and then slowly pour off the top 4 quarts. The last quart has the settled solids or contamination in it. 

I mix 3 gal of the clean used oil with gasoline nearly 1:1 in 5 gallon fuel cans and shake for 1 minute, and put two 5-gallons cans into a 3/4 full tank of diesel. My 2005 Ram diesel has a 40 gallon tank. This makes a mix of about 15% used oils.  It seems to run fine Winter and Summer.  I do change my fuel filter a little sooner than typical, about every 10,000 miles, rather than 15,000.

The dirty oil left in each jug i combine  again and let settle another 90 days and repeat. The 2nd time I pour off the top and use the remainder as chain-lube and make two-cycle fuel for my chainsaw, and some start my wood boiler at night.

It's a cheap and easy way to get rid of collected fluids, have a little free fuel, and not waste anything either.

text: If the oil has so much debris in it (and it often has if its been used a lot) that its about 1/4 sludge then   its gonna need filtering before adding the gelatine - either by letting it settle and draining above the sludge line or pouring through a filter. I would estimate roughly this method will only work if there is less than 10% solid swimming about in the oil so would be worth letting your oil settle for a week to see what you got because it might need filtering first before using the gelatine as a final cleaning.

author: @mch1969pt
text: I've driven about 180,000 miles on WVO. Firstly, its not without issues, so problem solving is a must. Secondly, it is better in an engine with indirect injection. This means an older - more inefficient engine that uses a Pinto style injector - (larger nozzles). The oil must still be pre-heated before going to the injector pump - so start up / shut down was always on ordinary diesel. I think that the same would apply to your mix of veg and paraffin. Technically you would also need to pay duty on the parafin as it isn't taxed for road fuel.  HMRC provide an exemption for alternative non fossil fuels allowing up to 2500 litres usage per year duty free. Use more than that and you pay duty tax on the lot! I used a centrifuge to clean the oil - it was very simple - pour the WVO into a drum through a fabric sieve (net curtain), draw the wvo from 6" off the bottom through a small gear pump (10L hr) into the centrifuge, where it then drops into a 40gal drum. I would change the filter and the bottom of the collection drum after six months, but my oil was always good quality. The centrifuge and gear pump operated on a timer - up to 2 hours so allowing me to prevent running the gear pump dry or overfilling the oil drum. A heating oil pump linked to a hose with a trigger nozzle allowed me to fill the car at the flick of a switch. When the tank filled, you could hear it gurgle, enabling you to stop before overflowing.

author: @jaixc
text:
I can agree with some of the commenters that this isn't actually "bio fuel" in the slightest. Because paraffin itself is a petroleum product, and you're just adding it to the oil to thin it out and to replace the need and use of kerosene as a blend, effectively considering this as cheap alternative to conventional fuels..

Paraffin is a type of liquified wax that has been extracted from petroleum, coal or shale. 

What Robert is doing is just following a recipe mixture that someone on the interwebs had tested and somewhat perfected which comes to about a 9:1 mixture of vegetable oil to paraffin being a replacement to kerosene. 

Most newer diesel fuels are more like a 60:40 mixture that is at the pumps, having kerosene blended into diesel for the allowance of engines to start and run at colder temps. Diesel also has to have napthenics and aromatics in commercial diesel by law, which don't burn as easily, and can eventually cause carbon buildup over time. 

A 50/30/10 (50 diesel/30 kerosene/10 paraffins) mixture is pretty close to be considered "premium diesel" having only a 2% loss in power over pure diesel, bringing down the costs per gallon. With that mixture, it'll burn cleaner, gain better fuel mileage, and provide less wear on the parts, and can be started easier in colder climates. Even the diesel fuel people run in their vehicles today has a paraffin/kerosene mixture that had been mixed commercially to meet emission standards, it's not premium by any means, especially at the pump. You're basically paying premium for a low grade bargain basement fuel.  

If you want to do some more research about it, the type of diesel fuel is called HVO, or renewable paraffinic diesel. It's not a new idea, but this mixture of vegetable oil and paraffin wax is a poor mans fuel if you're on the cheap, provided you get the vegetable oil really cheap or given to you, and the paraffin doesn't cost you a lot to purchase.

I personally wouldn't run this vegetable/paraffin mixture in anything legal transportation vehicle though. This would be decent to run in small diesel engines, and used as a lamp oil alternative. In my opinion, for you to be able to use vegetable oil in a newer post 2000+ vehicle you'd need to refine the oil and pull the fats from it, then mix it with methanol and non red kerosene, I'm pretty sure someone had figured it out with their blended biodiesel.

author: @51North
text:
I'd look at using petrol instead of paraffin. Much easier to buy in quantity and cheaper. It will have the same effect, but a petrol/biodiesel mix will be legal for road use, paraffin won't be. 

Years ago I used a mix of filtered used engine oil and petrol in some engines (non road) with many, many hours and petrol works as a great thinner for thick oils.

Might not work on very modern diesel engines, but old bullet proof diesels like the Peugeots mentioned run on anything if it's not too thick.

I took guidence from so old mercedes manuals that gave safe ratio's for mixing petrol with diesel for winter use and still be okay for the mechanical fuel pump, and can't remember exact numbers but you can go pretty big mix of petrol and it still be okay for the pump.

Explore with your own cautions however.

author: @kevinmcmuhammad4841
text: Thank you again Robert for another great video, but this is something that I have been doing for years to slow down my carbon footprint, and to save a few quid, so remember guys the tempature you store the waste cooking oil, will determine how good the refined product can be, if its too cold it will only wax up, and there are lots of different varieties of oil out there that tin out at different tempatures, please watch out also for rats, they will smell the food off the waste oil storeage cans " 5 gallon cans", and they will knaw at them and make a hole causing a leek into the ground and if it soaks into wood they will eat though the wood also, and when pouring it into your car, make sure you power wash around your filter housing and the hoses with detergent to get rid of the food smell from spilt oil to discourage the rats eating your fuel lines and other importand nessasary pasts needed to keep your vehicle functioning safely without causing a fire, also if you can get some black 5 gallon drums and leave them in a shed with a window and the sun shining on them, that will tin the oil down, making it easier to filter and less chance of injector blockages, I myself have been using waste cooking oil in my digger and generator and an old run about avensis and it never causes a problem, but always buy a few spare diesel filters, and have them handy for an emergency, you can also fit bigger capacity filters onto a lot of vehicals, the screw tread and width of the housing could be the same, it's just might have been supplied by manufacturers Toyota in my case with a smaller capacity filter to save on weight, not all vehicles will run on recycled oil, please do your research before trying it, but it's definitely worth doing, I've saved the price of a decent car from doing it,   Happy New Yeat

author: @TorquilBletchleySmythe
text: The main difference between this method and Sodium methoxide as far as I can see is the removal of glycerine and other products which clog injectors and foul seperators in modern diesel engines. The cracking of the triester which turns veg oil into 44 cetane minimum engine fuel is very important - without this, pre-combustion plastification at the injectors can easily occur as the glycerine still present in the oils is susceptible at engine operating temperatures. Sodium Methoxide, and diligent rinsing and settling produces truly usable fuel for motor vehicles, the method I see here does not address the gumming up issues WVO, or crude mixtures without catalytic reactions, seem to typefy. Indeed, not removing the glycerine is the source of much negative feedback from mechanics when it comes to using biofuels. If I have missed the part where the parrafin splits the triester into light, free-ester biofuel, would some kind person please point it out?

I saw a video where waste oil was put in a jug with a bottom drain. Looked like an upside down water cooler jug. It was outside in the sun. author: @kamaangirthearcher
text:
adding water and not removing it properly from the veg will wreck your injector pump and injectors in a very short period of time. Making "bio diesel" is not the same as blending, what is being demonstrated here, bio diesel is chemically different to veg oil by a process called trans esterification which reduces its viscosity to allow it to be run in more modern engines. 

It is illegal to run your vehicle for road use on any percentage of "other fuels" above  3-4% as additive. Bio fuels have a 2500 litre per year de-minus limit which is the allowance allowed for personal use (can't run a van on it for commercial use). 

You need to do a bit more research before posting misleading information that can have legal ramifications for people.

author: @roberttill3787
text: Robert what you are talking about is not biodiesel its actually waste vegetable oil, a different fuel source. The addition of kerosene is to thin the waste oil enough for it to pass through a diesel injector and ignite in a satisfactory manner. The reason an old Peugeot 206 runs on this is because the injector pump om that car will cope with waste oil (who knows for how long). Biodiesel is treated with all the chemicals you actually mention, and the oil is actually reacted into Biodiesel. The chemicals remove Glycerol from the oil which is the part of waste veg oil that damages the injector pump and clogs the filters and injectors. I usually like your videos, but in this one you are giving out information in a context that is totally untrue.

author: @joey5712
text: I realy need to clear up this common misconception. That is not biodiesel. Thats wvo(waste vegetable oil). It will run in the said car without paraffin, that just helps thin it. Eventually it will gum the rings.  Biodiesel is a fatty acid methyl ester FAME or FAEE (ethyl ester), this will not gum up your rings and depending on car will run in common rails upto 100%, WVO you will be lucky to get to 40/50% in a common rail and will probably knacker pump due to viscosity increase. WVO is a bio fuel not a Biodiesel.

author: @billguyan1913
text: Good interesting video. Some points: it's not biodiesel, which is fatty acid methyl ester, whereas veg oil is triglyceride. Both biodiesel and especially the paraffin thinned oil will clog almost all diesel particulate filters. Many people who run on biodiesel have their DPF's (illegally) removed. As for the Peugeot 206, the Pug/Citroen DPF FAP system is more tolerant of biodiesel. If it's an old 206 it might not have a dpf, but there's a danger of ring gumming. Personally, if I were running on thinned veg oil I'd add  petrol, not paraffin.

replies.author: @billguyan1913
replies.text: @patrickday4206  Petrol can't pre ignite in a diesel engine, there's no spark, so not pinging (pinking). If you use too much petrol or in very hot weather (which we rarely get in the UK) it's possible to have vapour lock. I ran a Suzuki Vitara (indirect engine, note) for years on oil thinned with 10% petrol with no issues.

author: @C-M-E
text:
I'm almost surprised they granted a patent for that. I helped a neighborhood guy convert a Merc diesel to WVO via the filter and 10% methanol treatment back in the early 90s and we were far from the 'first' guys to do it either. The 1970s were a boon in the US for finding cheaper ways to get usable fuel oils and I had some help from a book written then as well. 
Not that the gelatin is a waste of time or resources, but you can run the straight wvo (the 'cleanest' stuff we ever found consistently was from chinese food restaurants and yes, we'd get it free back then, otherwise the owner would have to pay a big outfit to come suck it out of a tank behind the building. Not free anymore as Robert said as people have caught on to the idea a lot more through the years, but hugely cheaper than going to the pump!) through remote filter units just using a little external 12v diesel pump, or if you're in a warmer climate, just dump in a handful of Alum when it's warm and let it naturally settle overnight in a large barrel. 
The friend I got going eventually started going to a few places with a 200+ gallon IBC tote and did a few batches back to back, then would have fuel for months. Sure smelled nice on the highway. 😁

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
For those in a hurry, put large batches inside of a spinning barrel [say if you get one of those old used washing machines which happens to have an amazingly small number of drain holes, so you can more easily plug them off, or perhaps line the tub with a thin sheet of polyester fiber and resin (I would never use fiberGLASS for this because it can break off and contaminate the product with abrasive glass if all goes badly, but polyester blanket is super soft and flexible, so it won't ever break off into your product] just to close off the gaps, remove the central agitator [unless you use it AS an agitator to do the heavy mixing for you], modify the switch to your new centrifuge so as to either alter or eliminate the little clock since it will remove your ability to put the unit on any cycle for more than a short period of time, and you probably want a longer period of time to spin or agitate, and then, disable the lid safety stop switch [its only going to be a problem for this anyhow] finally, set up a siphon tube to be lowered directly into the spinning vat so you can siphon off the skimmed oil at speed.

The reason for such a device is that you can do 20 or 30 gallons at a time with a decent sized washer [vertical axis only here, won't work on a horizontal one, for the most obvious of reasons], and, with a few additional considerations, can even be used in a continuous flow process much like an old cream separator.

That said, if you can just GET a cream separator, you have it made, because they are already continuous flow centrifuges.

I have looked at cross sections of cream separators, and, while they are indeed a great idea, would be pretty tough to just make at home, because they require a lot of very closely set, matching, balanced parts, and might not be cheap or easy to build from recycled bits, unless you just happen to come across some place which throws out copious amounts of uniform metal stampings of the right sizes and shapes.

But the general idea is that with centrifical forces, one could set up a constant flow-through process to pull off the waste and do so much more quickly than waiting for it to separate out by normal gravity, and one could avoid small batches which mean more work, or large static batches which mean much more of a wait time.

This would allow one to mix really large batches, and while one is staging, one can be separating out.

I have some 275 gallon HDPE tanks I got for various processes at my shop, and you can often get these used, either in food grade or petroleum grade [one I have came conveniently with 10 gallons of 5W-30 weight oil already in it, lucky me!] and with a decent opening at the top, and a valved opening at the bottom, it is easy to fill and drain them as long as you have them set to a workable height for the process, and you can stir them with a drop-in motor [they make such, of various types] or use a paint stirrer on the end of a hand drill.

If you go about setting up a whole bunch of oil to separate out, then you can slowly feed from the base of each tank into the separator device, so that it is always feeding in and always draining out at a fairly steady rate.

If one were really careful, one could build up the rim of a washer tub to have a spin-lip which would automatically allow spill-over of clean oil, and the dirty waste as 2 separate streams, but it would be a lot of work, and might require heavily modifying the washer body, and making some internal parts to place inside the barrel.

I admit, that the washing machine idea is sort of inferior to a normal cream separator [because of how much work and materials would be involved], but its larger, so it might be better for one who is looking to make a lot of fuel fast, over a very long period of time.

replies.text:
@newagetemplar6100 There is a way to use diatomaceous earth as an oil/fuel bleaching agent, and you can use certain kinds of clay for it as well.

The idea is that you force the oil compound through a really fine mesh level material which, in essence 'scrapes away' [filters] out the big junk.

Technically, you do not use a gelatin when using a molecular sieve though, as it will clog up if you do.

Typically, because there is a certain level of refractory value to your molecular sieve, you can regenerate it by roasting it, but if you get a cheap enough supply, it may not be worth the effort.

Also, where a high pressure pump would be a real bugger to mess with, centrifuging the oil through the sieve works as well.

The difference being, that instead of skimming the oil off of the crud, you spin the oil through the sieve and the crud stays at the top of the filtrate and can be dry-skimmed if one so desires.

One quick and easy way to pressure filter the oil, however, is to use the sand filter from a common swimming pool filtration system.

You just make sure you have the level of filter media that will clean your oil enough for the application.

If you are only using it for a heater, it can be pretty coarse, but if it is for a diesel fuel injector, it needs to be quite fine.

author: @ProlificInvention
text: A little tip for filtering oil for pretty much any purpose: They make 1 micron "filter bags" but they aren't the fastest method, but very effective.  So what I do is allow the oil to settle for a day, this allows the particulate contaminates and water to go to the bottom, I then pump out the top 80% or so to leave all that on bottom.  Then I put the filter bag in a section of PVC with a collar, fill with the oil, then I have a shop vac with the appropriate sized adapter to fit on the bottom of the PVC the filter is in.  What happens is the shop vac actively pulls the oil through the 1 micron filter quite quickly- basically a large poor man's Buchner funnel, filter and flask all in one.

author: @gurglejug627
text: The cost of used frying oils WVO varies around the UK from having to pay for it to being paid to take it away - the difference can be huge. I ran Diesel Engined Road vehicles on pure WVO for years, and it was sensible to have a heat exchanger to heat it up before it got into the injection pump as it as fairly viscose, and had a separate filter, as at some temperatures or with any sediment a filter could clog up - this obviously infers the need for a second fuel tank and a switchover system from one fuel to the other: We used Diesel to start and stop the engine to make it easy to start. It worked really well for years but effectiveness does vary from engine to engine - my Ford Transit was happy enough with it, as a Direct Injection Diesel (DI) engine, but my Citroen (Peugeot Diesel as standard) worked really well as it is an Indirect Injection Engine (IDI): Indeed with that Peugeot engone I got around 10-20% better economy PLUS some 20% more power, plus the engine was almost silent, even on a motorway - one heard the wind rushing over the car rather than hearing the engine. The only disadvantage to using WVO was the filtering effort, time and mess - and the need to heat it to filter, it except in summer. One thing to note was the additives we experimented with to get rid of/dissolve some floating 'other fats' on top of the bulk of the WVO - natural turpentine was best for this (not the chemical variant known as white spirit, though they look similar). We also had good results with paraffin, but I don't recall the exact results of the various experiments with it, (sorry to say) as we generally used some 97% pure WVO with a little paraffin and a very little Turpentine - we were limited in road use applications by regulations on the amount additives, by HMRC who were hot on it as the first people started to use it in numbers - I always felt this stymied research in typically 'British government' style. One is allowed to make a certain amount for private use these days - unless laws have changed from 15 years ago, this allows one to make one ton a year without registering with HMRC as a fuels producer as I was, so don't go off selling it openly ;) I really look forward to trying this new mix and at different temperatures to see if it is now practical for use ('filtering') as far north as we are now in Scandinavia. Please note that certain injector pumps such as Bosch were fine with WVO with its lubricity,  but other injector pumps don't like it (Lucas and others) and failed totally after a thousand miles or less: It will be interesting to hear how this 'new' fuel fares in that respect. For the record, pure WVO (even in the less efficient Ford Transit ID engine) showed zero particulate emissions at an MOT testing station at any revs - they thought their machine was broken until I swapped back onto Diesel: Technically, it also reduces several other harmful emissions, but raises one slightly - I have no idea how that would compare with a WVO/Paraffin mixture at 50%. Interestingly, when used in a wick lamp, paraffin burns very cleanly, but *cannot* be replaced by Diesel which soots them up very quickly and heavily - inferring that perhaps we ought to not be banning/extra taxing Diesel cars but instead using them on paraffin, perhaps with a little modification of fuel and/or vehicle, such as this one - could it really be this simple?! Thanks for the video :)

replies.author: @johnnodge4327
replies.text: Paraffin is very similar to kerosene and falls somewhere between kero and diesel by molecular weight. Paraffin is coloured red in most places for identification. This is normally due to it being taxed as a heating oil, rather than taxed as a road fuel.

replies.author: @gurglejug627
replies.text: weight or volume makes little odds - they are rather similar in weight (similar enough) and he is using volume measuring jugs in the video. Clear, liquid Paraffin was used all over the British Empire and in many countries as a heating and lighting fuel until some 30-40 years ago, but suddenly went out of use in many countries. It was rather cheap and had almost a universal commodity price - but now it varies massively from country to country, from around £1/litre in Norway to £4 a litre in Sweden, for example, last time I looked.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: in these terms paraffin, kerosene and lamp oil are basically the same thing mate - paraffin is the UK name, Kerosine the US name and lamp oil is a refined version - and yes I used 90% by volume veggie oil 10% by volume kerosene

replies.author: @RTR-MATEY
replies.text: 5 Gallon bucket. Drill 3/4" holes in perimeter of lid around edge. Cut hole in center and attach a *METAL* dryer hose flange with rivets. I already tried 3D printing in PET-G...worked for awhile but deformed due to higher heat than expected!!! Would have to 3D print in ABS but my 3D printer won't print that high of a temperature and I don't have an exhaust system setup. Once the lid is completed, fill bucket about 1/3rd full of water which helps to trap any lint that gets past your dryer lint filter. This device does two things. It reclaims the heat back in to your dwelling and it supplements your dry winter air with humidified air thus dramatically reducing respiratory issues!!! This setup will put out ALOT of humidity QUICKLY!!! You'll want to use a circulation fan to disperse the humidity to other areas of your home!!! Here where I live in Mississippi, it's ridiculously humid in the summer--gotta use an air conditioner constantly!!!...and in the winter it's crazy dry!!! I can run the central gas furnace but it doesn't get "comfortable" until I use my "swamp cooler" (evaporative cooler) to entrain moisture into the dry air. Only then does it become "comfortable"!!! I try to maintain humidity between 50-60%...anything less is uncomfortable and allows for static electricity!!! Can't allow that as I have various sensitive electronic devices around the house!!!
